post_text:
Every company has an AI strategy deck.

Fewer have an AI *usage* graph.

The gap between what you bought and what your teams actually run is where margin, momentum, and surprise live.

Every company bought the AI license.
Half are barely running inference.

A thread on the AI usage gap 🧵

2026 tech stack:
Postgres, Kubernetes, three copilots opened once in March,
and one person who still knows where the cron jobs live.

LLM didn't hallucinate.
It confidently described a department that dissolved in 2019.

We've all been there.

UK public sector AI:
beautiful principles document,
procurement cycle longer than the model's entire lifespan.

AI strategy deck: transformative.
Usage graph: opened twice, blamed intern, renewed anyway.

Every AI keynote: 'this changes everything.'
Every ops channel six months later: 'who owns the API key?'

US enterprise AI playbook:
$2M contract → 12-person pilot → reorg → dashboard green, usage somewhere else.

Germany: engineering-grade caution on AI.
Also engineering-grade Excel exports nobody audits.

Honest question — no vendor pitch:

Where have you actually seen AI work outside a demo?
Office, farm, hospital, warehouse — curious what's real.

GPU shortage solved the wrong problem.
We still can't explain what half the models are doing on Tuesdays.

Precision ag pitch: every plant individually monitored.
Field reality: one sensor offline, dashboard says 🟢, farmer trusts the neighbor's rain feeling.

Hot take: the AI rollout isn't failing in the demo.

It's failing in week 9 — when the pilot team moves on, the API key stays active, and nobody checks the dashboard.

Usage telemetry > launch announcement.

Open-source model drops are like free puppies.
Adorable announcement. Someone still feeds it GPUs at 2am.

Every AI keynote: 'this changes everything.'
Every ops channel six months later: 'who owns the API key?'

Netherlands: 11 apps to book a dentist,
one AI that summarizes why you still didn't go.

ChatGPT for emails: great.
ChatGPT for legal, finance, and HR without review: see you in discovery.

Tech Twitter when a new benchmark drops:
📈 numbers go up
🧍 production workflows unchanged

UK public sector AI:
beautiful principles document,
procurement cycle longer than the model's entire lifespan.

Your inference bill is a honesty meter.

Spike = people found a workflow that works.
Flatline = shelfware with a logo on it.

Tokens don't care about your press release.

The most underrated AI feature is still 'Ctrl+Z'
because the model already emailed three clients.

Developed countries fund ag AI with grants and pilots.
Farmers fund intuition until the intuition is wrong — then both learn.

Developed countries don't lack AI tools.
They lack boring owners for boring workflows after go-live.

What's the first sign AI adoption is fading?

🔴 Inference decay
🟡 Workflow owner left
🟢 Active users dropping
⚫ 'We're still exploring use cases'

Robots in warehouses: impressive.
Robots in slide decks: undefeated.

Japan's factory AI adoption is underrated.
Less keynote. More 'this line stops costing us money.'

EU AI Act conversations: serious, necessary, slow.
Meanwhile teams still share API keys in a Slack thread named 'temp'.

Pilot team mood: 🚀
Week 9 ops mood: 🫠
Finance mood: ✅ renewed

AI safety discourse is important.
So is the mundane safety of not piping prod data into a chat box.

Most AI rollouts die after the demo video.

Here's the ops playbook that keeps inference alive 🧵

2/ The 85% usage rule.

Actual consumption / licensed spend < 0.85?
Something changed in how the company runs AI.
Find out what before the next budget cycle.

Ag tech AI and enterprise AI share a problem:
beautiful dashboard, suspiciously quiet API.

My plants tomorrow.
Same energy: everyone wants capacity, nobody wants maintenance.

AI drone maps the field.
Model flags blight.
Farmer checks the corner that always lies to the algorithm.

Plot twist: the company didn't cancel AI.

They just stopped calling it.

Contract still active ✅
Dashboard still green ✅
Inference down 46% over 6 months ❌

Different problem. Different fix.

Nordic AI governance: thoughtful whitepaper, calm committee,
then someone runs the real workflow in a spreadsheet anyway.

Smart irrigation sold the dream of perfect water math.
Reality: a clogged line and a very human Saturday.

Drop the AI tool your company bought but barely uses.

I'll start: enterprise platform, -46% inference over 6 months, internal replacement built quietly. Dashboard still green. 💀

AI strategy deck: transformative.
Usage graph: opened twice, blamed intern, renewed anyway.

Germany: engineering-grade caution on AI.
Also engineering-grade Excel exports nobody audits.

📊 AI usage telemetry (demo portfolio, real math):

Licensed spend:  $2.5M
Actual usage:    $2.11M run-rate
Idle gap:        $390K
Fading:          2 of 5

15.6% of your AI budget might be ambient noise.

Smart irrigation sold the dream of perfect water math.
Reality: a clogged line and a very human Saturday.

Honest question for ops leaders:

Does your AI spend match actual usage?

📊 Yes — we track inference
😬 Sometimes — we catch it late
💀 No idea — we track the contract

Japan's factory AI adoption is underrated.
Less keynote. More 'this line stops costing us money.'

🏭 Company story (demo data, anonymized):

Media company. Big AI platform contract.
This is what fading adoption actually looks like 🧵

Month 1: $520K contract. 100 inference units. 100 seats.
Month 6: 54 units. 41 active users.

Ops dashboard: 🟢 fine
Support volume: high
Workflow owner: gone

ChatGPT for emails: great.
ChatGPT for legal, finance, and HR without review: see you in discovery.

Netherlands: 11 apps to book a dentist,
one AI that summarizes why you still didn't go.

The team didn't quit AI loudly.
They just... routed around it.

Internal tool absorbed the workflow.
API calls dropped. Nobody updated the runbook.

Registry check: legal entity flagged dissolved.

Contract still running.
Inference still decaying.
Budget line still full.

Three dashboards. Three stories.

Singapore treats AI like infrastructure.
A lot of places treat it like confetti for the annual report.

The lesson:

Buying AI ≠ operating AI.
The contract renews. The workflow quietly leaves.
Someone should be watching that. Often nobody is.

Yield prediction models love clean data.
Soil loves surprises. Marriage of inconvenience.

Big ag tech demos in climate-controlled rooms.
Small farms debug in mud with gloves on. Different test environment.

Developed countries don't lack AI tools.
They lack boring owners for boring workflows after go-live.
